_meta.authoringNotes: Verified against AAO BCSC Glaucoma (Section 10) 2022-2023, Costa & Arcieri Surv Ophthalmol 2007 hypotony review (PMID 17822740), Schubert hypotony review Surv Ophthalmol 1996/2011 follow-ups, ICD-10 H44.4/H44.40 mapping. Atropine, autologous blood injection, scleral patch, and direct cyclopexy doses/protocols cross-checked against AAO EyeWiki entries and current peer-reviewed surgical series.

h1Title: Ocular Hypotony: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Ocular hypotony is intraocular pressure at or below 5 mmHg, low enough that the eye begins to lose its structural support and, if sustained, develops fluid in the choroid, folds across the macula, and swelling of the optic nerve head. It most often follows glaucoma filtering surgery, cataract surgery with a leaking wound, blunt eye trauma that tears the ciliary body, or chronic uveitis that shuts down aqueous production. Patients notice blurry vision, soft eye-feel, occasional flashing lights, and in some cases a shallow front chamber visible to the clinician within hours. Persistent hypotony for weeks risks irreversible hypotony maculopathy and, in the worst case, a shrunken non-functional eye (phthisis bulbi). Most cases respond to stopping pressure-lowering drops, atropine, and a graded sequence of bleb revision, autologous blood injection, scleral patch grafting, or direct cyclopexy.

keyStats.prevalence: Occurs in 4-20% of trabeculectomies and 2-5% of tube-shunt cases in the first 3 months (Costa 2007 review; AGIS subgroup data)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in post-glaucoma-surgery patients, young myopes after filtering surgery (3-5x higher hypotony maculopathy risk), and adults after penetrating eye trauma
keyStats.avgAge: Two peaks: 30-50 years (trauma, young myopic glaucoma patients) and 65-80 years (post-cataract and post-trabeculectomy)
keyStats.globalCases: No precise global registry; estimated 50,000-100,000 incident cases annually worldwide following filtration surgery alone

definition: Ocular hypotony (ICD-10: H44.4; unspecified H44.40) is defined statistically as intraocular pressure of 5 mmHg or lower (more than three standard deviations below the population mean of 15.5 mmHg). It is defined clinically by the appearance of structural consequences: shallowing of the anterior chamber, ciliochoroidal effusion, optic disc edema, and macular folds known as hypotony maculopathy. The mechanism is either reduced aqueous humor production by an injured or inflamed ciliary body, or excessive aqueous outflow through a surgical fistula, wound leak, cyclodialysis cleft, or retinal detachment. Hypotony is staged by duration (transient under 1 month, persistent 1-3 months, chronic beyond 3 months) and by anatomic effect (numerical hypotony alone versus hypotony maculopathy with vision loss). It is managed by ophthalmologists, with glaucoma subspecialists handling most post-surgical cases and vitreoretinal surgeons stepping in when traction, chronic ciliary detachment, or vitrectomy revision is required. Untreated chronic hypotony progresses through optic disc swelling, choroidal folds, retinal striae, and ultimately phthisis bulbi — a soft, shrunken, blind eye that is the end-stage of a long list of ocular insults.

typesClassification.description: Intraocular pressure of 5 mmHg or less without anatomic consequences. The eye remains structurally normal, vision is preserved, and the condition may resolve without intervention.

Low pressure plus visible structural change — retinal folds radiating from the fovea, choroidal effusions, optic disc swelling, or shallow anterior chamber. Vision is typically affected and intervention is required.
Develops within the first month after filtering surgery, tube shunt placement, or cataract surgery. Often due to wound leak or overfiltration; usually responsive to conservative measures.

Persists beyond 3 months. Caused by chronic overfiltering blebs, unrecognized cyclodialysis cleft, ciliary body shutdown from uveitis or trauma, or chronic retinal detachment. Risk of permanent maculopathy and phthisis rises sharply.

End-stage hypotony with a shrunken, disorganized, painful or non-functional eye. IOP is unmeasurably low; the eye is firm, fibrotic, and beyond visual recovery. Often follows severe trauma, untreated chronic hypotony, or end-stage uveitis.

primarySymptoms: Blurred or distorted central vision that fluctuates with head position, often most pronounced in the first weeks after glaucoma or cataract surgery.
Sudden drop in best-corrected visual acuity by 2 or more Snellen lines in the affected eye, particularly when hypotony maculopathy with macular folds develops.
Photopsias — brief flashes of light at the periphery of vision that often signal choroidal effusion or vitreoretinal traction from the soft globe.
A soft or sunken feeling in the eye, sometimes accompanied by a sense that the eyelid is heavier than usual when blinking.
Mild aching or pressure-like discomfort around the eye and brow; severe pain is uncommon and should prompt evaluation for endophthalmitis or suprachoroidal hemorrhage.
Watering or epiphora when a wound leak is present, with tears mixing with aqueous fluid that escapes through the leak.
Frontal headache that may improve when lying flat, mirroring the reversal of papilledema in raised intracranial pressure.
Metamorphopsia — straight lines appearing wavy on an Amsler grid — reflecting retinal folds and choroidal undulation at the macula.
Reduced contrast sensitivity and difficulty reading in low light, even when central acuity remains preserved.
Halos or starburst patterns around lights in chronic hypotony with mild corneal edema or anterior chamber shallowing.
earlyWarningSigns: A bleb that appears unusually large, thin-walled, or visibly draining on slit-lamp examination after trabeculectomy
Anterior chamber depth that has measurably decreased between two consecutive postoperative visits
New optic disc fullness on dilated examination, even before central vision is affected
Asymptomatic IOP readings of 4-5 mmHg on routine post-surgical Goldmann tonometry
Persistent low-grade aqueous flare following blunt eye trauma, suggesting subclinical ciliary body injury
emergencySigns: Sudden severe eye pain with loss of vision — raises suspicion of suprachoroidal hemorrhage, endophthalmitis, or large choroidal detachment requiring same-day specialist review
Visible tear or full-thickness eye wound with leaking fluid after trauma or surgery — needs urgent ophthalmic repair within hours
Acute drop in vision to counting fingers or worse in an eye with known hypotony — may signal hypotony maculopathy progressing or retinal detachment
Hypopyon (layered white cells in the anterior chamber) or severe pain in a recently operated hypotonic eye — endophthalmitis until proven otherwise
New diplopia or marked proptosis combined with hypotony — raises suspicion of orbital pathology or scleral rupture

causes.description: A self-sealing cataract incision, clear corneal wound, or trabeculectomy conjunctival flap that fails to close lets aqueous escape directly to the surface. Common in the first 1-4 weeks after surgery and is the most frequent cause of early postoperative hypotony.

The filtering fistula drains aqueous faster than the ciliary body produces it. Risk is highest in young, myopic eyes and in cases where mitomycin-C was used to prevent scarring. Bleb appears thin-walled, diffuse, and avascular.

Early overdrainage through a non-valved Baerveldt or Molteno tube, or a valved Ahmed valve with a failed restrictor, allows continuous aqueous loss. Most common in the first 6 weeks before fibrous encapsulation forms around the plate.

A separation of the ciliary body from the scleral spur creates a direct channel from the anterior chamber to the suprachoroidal space. Usually caused by blunt eye trauma; occasionally iatrogenic during anterior segment surgery.

A full-thickness eye wall injury allows immediate decompression. Even after primary repair, chronic hypotony can persist if the ciliary body was injured or if a hidden posterior wound continues to leak.

causes.cause: Chronic anterior uveitis with ciliary body shutdown
causes.description: Persistent intraocular inflammation in conditions such as juvenile idiopathic arthritis-associated uveitis, sarcoidosis, sympathetic ophthalmia, or HLA-B27 disease damages the non-pigmented ciliary epithelium, reducing aqueous production permanently.

causes.cause: Retinal detachment, especially chronic or total
causes.description: Detached retina shifts aqueous outflow dynamics and disrupts the blood-aqueous barrier. Up to 25% of long-standing rhegmatogenous retinal detachments are associated with measurable hypotony at presentation.

Stacking multiple aqueous-suppressant drops — a beta-blocker, alpha-agonist, and carbonic anhydrase inhibitor — in an eye that has had filtering surgery, trauma, or uveitis can drop pressure below 5 mmHg. Recognizing and reducing the regimen is often curative.
Eyes with longstanding optic nerve damage or chronic uveitis sometimes have reduced aqueous production at baseline; adding even one IOP-lowering agent can precipitate hypotony in this group.

riskFactors.description: Mitomycin-C and 5-fluorouracil are used during filtering surgery to prevent scarring but raise hypotony risk by 2-3 fold. Concentration, application time, and surgeon technique modify the risk profile.

Long, thin scleras transmit traction more easily and develop hypotony maculopathy at higher pressures. Myopic eyes show maculopathy at IOPs that a non-myopic eye would tolerate without visible damage.

Younger eyes have a more elastic sclera and a brisker healing-and-scarring response, paradoxically associated with both early overfiltration and later bleb failure. Hypotony maculopathy risk is roughly 3-5x higher than in patients over 60.

Closed-globe injuries from sports, airbags, or assault can produce a cyclodialysis cleft that may not be obvious for weeks. Any unexplained chronic hypotony after trauma warrants gonioscopy to look for a cleft.

Diseases such as juvenile idiopathic arthritis-associated uveitis, sarcoidosis, and idiopathic chronic iridocyclitis cause progressive ciliary epithelial damage. Long-term IOP can drift well below 10 mmHg and approach hypotony levels.

Non-valved devices (Baerveldt, Molteno) require tube ligation and a hypotony-prevention strategy. Failure of these intraoperative safeguards is a major cause of early postoperative hypotony in tube cases.
Absence of the lens and vitreous loss disrupt anterior segment fluid dynamics and slightly increase the risk of post-cataract hypotony, especially if the posterior capsule was ruptured.
Advanced proliferative diabetic retinopathy with traction can cause chronic hypotony through ciliary body detachment and reduced aqueous production. Vitrectomy alters this risk in either direction.

riskFactors.factor: Prior penetrating keratoplasty or anterior segment reconstruction
riskFactors.description: Multiple anterior segment surgeries thin the conjunctiva and weaken healing, increasing wound-leak risk after subsequent filtering procedures.

riskFactors.factor: Concurrent use of multiple aqueous suppressants
riskFactors.description: Eyes already on a beta-blocker, alpha-agonist, and carbonic anhydrase inhibitor have very low baseline aqueous production. Any additional insult (mild trauma, inflammation, recent surgery) easily tips them into hypotony.

affectedDemographics: Post-trabeculectomy patients: 4-20% develop measurable hypotony in the first 3 months; 1-7% develop hypotony maculopathy (Costa 2007 review of pooled series)
Post tube-shunt patients: 2-5% experience early hypotony with valved devices; non-valved tubes have higher rates before ligature dissolution
Young myopic patients: 3-5x higher rate of hypotony maculopathy after filtering surgery than older non-myopes
Trauma victims: cyclodialysis cleft is identified in 1-3% of closed-globe ocular trauma referrals to tertiary centers
Chronic uveitis cohorts: long-term hypotony develops in 5-15% over a 10-year follow-up, depending on disease type and treatment intensity
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines a measured IOP of 5 mmHg or less with a structured anterior and posterior segment examination that identifies which mechanism is at fault. Goldmann applanation tonometry remains the reference standard, although in soft hypotonic eyes a Tono-Pen or rebound tonometer is sometimes more practical because the cornea is too irregular for accurate applanation. Once low pressure is confirmed, the next decision is whether the eye is simply numerically hypotonic with a quiet exam or is showing anatomic consequences — shallow anterior chamber, choroidal effusions, optic disc swelling, retinal folds emanating from the fovea — that define hypotony maculopathy and require intervention. The Seidel test, performed by painting fluorescein over the surgical wound and watching for a dilution stream under cobalt blue light, identifies a wound leak in under a minute. Gonioscopy is essential after blunt trauma to look for a cyclodialysis cleft, which appears as a dark sliver between the scleral spur and the ciliary body. Optical coherence tomography of the macula visualizes choroidal folds and chorioretinal striae that confirm hypotony maculopathy and provides a baseline against which treatment response is measured. B-scan ultrasound documents the size and chronicity of choroidal effusions or detachments, especially when the fundus is hard to see because of cataract, hyphema, or vitreous opacity. Anterior segment OCT or ultrasound biomicroscopy can confirm a suspected cyclodialysis cleft when gonioscopy is non-diagnostic. Differential diagnosis includes phthisis bulbi (end-stage with a firm shrunken eye, IOP unrecordable), papilledema from raised intracranial pressure (disc swelling but normal or high IOP), and chronic retinal detachment (low IOP secondary to detachment rather than primary outflow problem). When the cause is not immediately obvious, the workflow is to repeat IOP, examine the bleb with Seidel testing, perform gonioscopy, and image the macula and choroid — these four steps establish a diagnosis in the great majority of cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference-standard measurement of intraocular pressure to confirm hypotony (IOP ≤5 mmHg) and to follow response to treatment. The cornea must be regular enough to applanate; in highly irregular corneas a Tono-Pen or iCare rebound tonometer is substituted.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A drop of fluorescein and topical anesthetic is placed in each eye; the patient rests their chin on the slit lamp and a small prism gently touches the cornea for a few seconds. The reading is painless and takes under a minute per eye.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies an aqueous leak through a surgical wound or thin bleb. Even a microscopic leak that is not visible on direct inspection is unmasked by the dilution stream of clear aqueous through the fluorescein dye.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fluorescein strip is wetted and applied over the suspect area. Under cobalt blue light at the slit lamp the examiner watches for 30-60 seconds; a green stream of dilution confirms a leak.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes the anterior chamber angle to look for a cyclodialysis cleft — a tell-tale dark slit between the scleral spur and the ciliary body. Especially important after blunt trauma or unexplained chronic hypotony.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After topical anesthetic, a small contact lens with mirrors is rested on the cornea. The examiner inspects all four quadrants of the angle. The procedure is painless and takes 2-3 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) of the macula
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents hypotony maculopathy — choroidal folds, retinal striae radiating from the fovea, and any subretinal fluid. Provides a quantitative baseline (central macular thickness, fold pattern) that is used to monitor response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient sits at a non-contact imaging device, fixates on an internal target, and the scan completes in under a minute per eye. No drops needed for most modern OCTs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps choroidal effusions or detachments behind the iris and demonstrates the configuration of the posterior segment when the view through the pupil is obscured. Differentiates serous effusion from hemorrhagic detachment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A coupling gel is applied to the closed eyelid and a small probe is pressed gently while the patient looks in different directions. The scan takes 5-10 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Anterior segment OCT or ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM)
diagnosticTests.purpose: High-resolution imaging of the anterior chamber angle and ciliary body. UBM in particular confirms a cyclodialysis cleft when gonioscopy is equivocal, and quantifies its circumferential extent for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: For anterior segment OCT, a non-contact scan at the slit lamp. For UBM, a small fluid-filled cup rests on the surface of the eye after topical anesthetic; the patient lies back for 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies optic disc swelling, choroidal folds, retinal detachment, and the broader status of the posterior segment. A baseline disc photo is useful for follow-up.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dilating drops are instilled; vision is blurred for 4-6 hours afterward. The exam itself takes 5-10 minutes with a bright headlight and a handheld lens.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates leakage at the disc and choroidal vasculature pattern in atypical hypotony maculopathy or when papilledema-versus-disc-edema-from-hypotony is in doubt.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fluorescein dye is injected into an arm vein; serial photographs are taken over 5-10 minutes. A transient yellow skin and orange urine discoloration is normal for 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of ocular hypotony has a clear ladder. The first step is to remove every reversible contributor: stop all aqueous-suppressant drops, taper unnecessary anti-inflammatories that may also reduce production, and protect the eye from external pressure with a shield. The second step is medical — cycloplegia with atropine 1% three times daily deepens the anterior chamber, reduces ciliary spasm, and reverses the ciliary-body forward rotation that drives choroidal effusion. Topical and oral corticosteroids treat the underlying uveitic component when inflammation is contributing. A pressure patch or a Simmons shell (a curved scleral shell that mechanically tamponades the wound) is added for wound leaks at the surface. If conservative measures fail, the third step is procedural: bleb revision with conjunctival resuturing for an overfiltering trabeculectomy, autologous blood injection into the bleb to induce a sterile inflammatory closure, scleral patch graft (donor sclera or pericardium) for refractory thin or leaking blebs, and direct cyclopexy or argon laser to the cleft for symptomatic cyclodialysis. Tube-shunt overdrainage is managed by ligation or tube extension. Vitrectomy is reserved for chronic hypotony where vitreous traction or persistent ciliary detachment will not respond to anterior segment maneuvers; this is most often a vitreoretinal-glaucoma combined approach in advanced cases. Guidelines from the American Academy of Ophthalmology BCSC Glaucoma section recommend a stepwise approach: confirm and localize the mechanism, give conservative measures 1-2 weeks where vision is preserved, and escalate quickly to surgical revision when hypotony maculopathy is present or progressing. The goal is restoration of pressure into the low-teens range with reversal of choroidal effusion and resolution of macular folds. Visual recovery is usually substantial when intervention occurs within 3 months; chronic hypotony beyond 6 months risks permanent maculopathy and progression toward phthisis bulbi. Multidisciplinary input — glaucoma specialist, vitreoretinal surgeon, and uveitis service when relevant — produces the best outcomes in complex cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Discontinuation of all aqueous-suppressant drops
medicalTreatments.description: Beta-blockers (timolol), alpha-agonists (brimonidine), carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (dorzolamide, brinzolamide, oral acetazolamide), and prostaglandin analogues are stopped in the affected eye. This single step resolves hypotony in 15-25% of post-surgical cases where medication stacking was a contributor.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves hypotony in 15-25% of postoperative cases as a sole intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Atropine 1% three times daily
medicalTreatments.description: Long-acting cycloplegic that deepens the anterior chamber, prevents ciliary body forward rotation, and reduces choroidal effusion. First-line topical medical therapy for hypotony with shallow chamber or choroidal effusion in nearly every published protocol.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Deepens anterior chamber and reverses early choroidal effusion in 60-80% of cases when started within 2 weeks of onset.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical and oral corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% hourly, oral prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces uveitic inflammation that may be contributing to ciliary body shutdown, and dampens postoperative inflammation that fuels overfiltration. Used cautiously in patients with infection risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Helps restore aqueous production in inflammation-driven hypotony; specific response rates vary from 40-70% depending on uveitis subtype.

medicalTreatments.name: Pressure patch and Simmons shell
medicalTreatments.description: External tamponade for wound leaks and overfiltering blebs. The pressure patch is a firm cotton dressing; the Simmons shell is a curved scleral lens that selectively compresses the bleb without occluding the cornea. Usually maintained for 24-72 hours and reassessed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Closes 60-75% of small early wound leaks and reduces overfiltration in 40-60% of bleb-related hypotony when used early.

medicalTreatments.name: Autologous blood injection into the bleb
medicalTreatments.description: 0.1-0.2 mL of the patient's venous blood is injected into the wall of an overfiltering bleb to induce a sterile inflammatory response that thickens and seals it. Performed at the slit lamp under topical anesthetic.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves hypotony in 50-75% of selected overfiltering trabeculectomy blebs; may require repeat injections.

medicalTreatments.name: Aqueous suppressant withdrawal plus oral fluid restriction (rare adjunct)
medicalTreatments.description: For carefully selected refractory cases with severe choroidal effusion, brief fluid restriction is occasionally combined with steroid and atropine therapy. Not commonly used; reserved for inpatient settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of underlying uveitis
medicalTreatments.description: Disease-specific immunomodulation — methotrexate, mycophenolate, adalimumab for JIA-associated uveitis, sarcoidosis, or HLA-B27 disease — reduces inflammatory injury to the ciliary epithelium and can stabilize chronic hypotony.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilizes or partially reverses hypotony in 30-60% of uveitis-driven cases over 6-12 months.

surgicalOptions.name: Conjunctival resuturing for wound leak
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical re-approximation of the conjunctival flap over a leaking trabeculectomy site, or watertight closure of a leaking cataract wound. Performed at the slit lamp under topical anesthesia or in the operating room for larger defects.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolves the leak and restores pressure in 80-90% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Bleb revision with compression sutures or transconjunctival sutures
surgicalOptions.description: Reinforcement of an overfiltering bleb using compression sutures or transconjunctival sutures that narrow the filtering pathway. Adjustable in the early postoperative period.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces overfiltration in 60-80% of cases; some loss of pressure control may require subsequent therapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Scleral patch graft over the bleb
surgicalOptions.description: Donor sclera, pericardium, or cornea is sewn over a thin or leaking bleb and re-covered with conjunctiva. Provides durable structural support to a failing bleb wall.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Closes the leak and resolves hypotony in 70-85% of refractory cases; some loss of filtering function is acceptable.

surgicalOptions.name: Autologous blood injection into the bleb
surgicalOptions.description: Office-based procedure described above under medical treatments — the line between medical and procedural treatment is blurred. Used as the lowest-morbidity surgical option before formal revision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 50-75% resolution of hypotony in suitable blebs; may be repeated.

surgicalOptions.name: Direct cyclopexy for cyclodialysis cleft
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical re-attachment of the ciliary body to the scleral spur. Performed when argon laser, transscleral diathermy, or cryotherapy have failed to close a symptomatic cleft. Can be open (ab-externo) or anterior-segment (ab-interno).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Closes the cleft and restores pressure in 75-90% of cases; an IOP spike after closure is common and is usually managed medically.

surgicalOptions.name: Argon laser cyclophotocoagulation to a cyclodialysis cleft
surgicalOptions.description: Laser energy applied to the edges of a small or moderate cleft induces scarring and closure. First-line for small clefts before direct cyclopexy is considered.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Closes clefts of less than 3 clock-hours in 50-70% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Tube ligation or tube revision for shunt overdrainage
surgicalOptions.description: An overdrainage glaucoma drainage device is treated by external ligature, internal stent placement, or partial occlusion of the tube to slow flow until fibrous encapsulation forms around the plate.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restores pressure to physiologic range in 70-85% of cases of tube-related hypotony.

surgicalOptions.name: Pars plana vitrectomy with gas or oil tamponade
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for chronic hypotony with vitreous traction on the ciliary body, large hemorrhagic choroidal detachment, or coexistent retinal detachment. Combined glaucoma-retina operating teams handle these cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restoration of measurable IOP in 50-70% of advanced cases; visual recovery is variable and depends on chronicity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Eye-shield protection during sleep and risk activities
alternativeTreatments.description: A perforated plastic or metal shield reduces external pressure on a soft eye, lowering the risk of secondary trauma. Evidence base is observational but uniformly recommended by AAO guidelines after filtering surgery and during active hypotony.

alternativeTreatments.name: Patient education and avoidance of Valsalva activity
alternativeTreatments.description: Avoiding heavy lifting, intense straining, and contact sports during the early postoperative period reduces episodes of acute outflow that can deepen hypotony. Best regarded as a supportive measure, not a treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Hypotony is managed by ophthalmologists, with glaucoma specialists handling most cases that follow filtering surgery or tube-shunt placement and vitreoretinal surgeons stepping in when chronic ciliary detachment, vitreous traction, or retinal detachment is part of the picture. Specialist referral within 1-2 weeks is warranted whenever vision is dropping, hypotony maculopathy is suspected on OCT, or conservative measures have not raised pressure into the high single digits.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in ophthalmology with subspecialty fellowship in glaucoma or vitreoretinal surgery. Ask about case volume in trabeculectomy revision, autologous blood injection, cyclopexy, and scleral patch grafting. Centers with combined glaucoma-retina services produce the best outcomes for complex chronic hypotony. Continuity matters; many of these eyes need repeat assessment and staged intervention over months.
preventionStrategies: Use lower antimetabolite concentrations and shorter application times during trabeculectomy in young or myopic eyes, where hypotony maculopathy risk is highest
Ligate non-valved tube shunts intraoperatively with an absorbable suture to delay flow until fibrous encapsulation forms around the plate, typically 4-6 weeks
Review the postoperative medication list at each visit and stop any aqueous suppressant that is no longer required once a filtering procedure has lowered IOP
Counsel patients to avoid Valsalva activities, eye rubbing, and contact sports for 4-6 weeks after filtering surgery and to wear a shield at night
Screen all post-trauma patients with persistent low IOP using gonioscopy and anterior segment imaging to detect cyclodialysis cleft before maculopathy develops
Maintain tight inflammation control in chronic uveitis with systemic immunomodulators to prevent slow ciliary body shutdown
lifestyleModifications: Wear a protective shield at night and during physical activity throughout any active hypotony episode and for at least 4 weeks after surgical revision
Avoid swimming and hot tubs while a wound leak or thin bleb is present, to reduce infection risk in a soft eye
Limit heavy lifting (over 10 kg) and high-intensity straining exercise for 4-6 weeks after any filtering procedure
Sleep with the head slightly elevated for the first 7-10 days after surgery to reduce orbital venous congestion and bleb stress
Inform every clinician of the hypotony history before any future eye examination; even routine applanation in a soft eye requires care
Stop or reduce systemic medications that can promote uveitis (bisphosphonates, certain checkpoint inhibitors) only in consultation with the prescribing physician
Use lubricating drops to manage surface discomfort in the days after surgery, but avoid preservative-laden drops on a healing wound when possible

recommended: Adequate daily fluid intake (1.5-2 liters of water unless restricted) to support stable systemic blood pressure and ocular perfusion
Mediterranean-style diet rich in green leafy vegetables, fish, and olive oil — supports vascular and inflammatory health in chronic uveitis cohorts
Vitamin A and zinc from food sources (eggs, dairy, oily fish) to maintain corneal epithelial integrity during healing
Vitamin C and citrus fruits in moderation to support wound healing without disrupting any IOP management
Reasonable protein intake (0.8-1.2 g/kg/day) to support post-surgical tissue repair
avoid: Excessive caffeine intake (over 400 mg/day) in the early postoperative phase, as the systemic vascular effects can worsen ocular perfusion swings
High-salt processed foods that can drive blood pressure variability and choroidal effusion
Heavy alcohol intake in the first 2 weeks after surgery — impairs wound healing and adherence
Excessive vitamin A supplementation (>10,000 IU daily long-term), which is unnecessary and carries its own risks
Strict prolonged fasting in the early postoperative period — dehydration adds stress to a soft eye

exerciseGuidelines: Avoid heavy lifting, contact sports, and high-impact exercise for at least 4-6 weeks after any filtering surgery and during active hypotony. Gentle walking is encouraged. Swimming, scuba diving, and hot tubs are off-limits until the conjunctiva is sealed and IOP is stable. Resumption of full activity follows the surgeon's assessment, typically 6-12 weeks after a successful revision.
dailyManagement: Wear a protective shield at night and during activities that risk eye contact
Instill atropine 1% three times daily exactly as prescribed; missed doses prolong the recovery
Photograph the eye weekly with a phone camera in good light — the patient and clinician can compare bleb size and conjunctival appearance between visits
Track best-corrected vision with a near card at home; a drop of two lines warrants prompt review
Keep follow-up appointments — every 1-2 weeks during the first 6 weeks of active management, then taper based on stability
Carry a card or app note listing current medications, allergies, and the operating surgeon's contact for unexpected acute issues
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the cause and the duration of hypotony before intervention. Early postoperative wound leaks treated within 2 weeks recover vision in over 90% of patients. Hypotony maculopathy diagnosed and reversed within 3 months recovers central vision substantially in 60-80% of cases, though some residual metamorphopsia is common when retinal folds were present. Chronic hypotony beyond 6 months has a poorer outlook; only 30-50% of patients regain useful central vision and a small minority progress to phthisis bulbi requiring enucleation or evisceration for comfort. Cyclodialysis clefts closed surgically often produce a transient IOP spike that needs medical management; long-term pressure control after closure is generally good. Visual recovery after cleft closure is typically substantial when the eye has been hypotonic for less than 6 months. The decisive prognostic factor is time to diagnosis and intervention. Lifelong follow-up by an ophthalmologist is appropriate even after successful recovery, because recurrence and late maculopathy are well-described and need early detection.
recoveryTimeline: Pressure typically rises within 24-72 hours of stopping aqueous suppressants and starting atropine in cooperative early postoperative hypotony. Choroidal effusions resolve over 1-3 weeks with conservative care. Hypotony maculopathy reverses on OCT over 4-12 weeks once normal pressure is restored, with the bulk of visual recovery occurring in the first 3 months. After surgical revision (bleb resuturing, scleral patch, cyclopexy), pressure stabilizes within 1-2 weeks and full visual recovery may take 3-6 months. Patients with chronic hypotony beyond 6 months often see partial rather than complete visual recovery and require staged interventions over 6-12 months.
complications: Hypotony maculopathy with permanent retinal folds and metamorphopsia — the most common visually relevant complication; risk rises sharply beyond 3 months of sustained IOP under 6 mmHg
Choroidal detachment (serous or hemorrhagic) — large effusions can cause kissing choroidals and require surgical drainage when retina-on-retina contact develops
Suprachoroidal hemorrhage — a rare but devastating complication, presents with sudden severe pain and vision loss; risk is highest in elderly, anticoagulated, or arteriopathic patients
Endophthalmitis through a wound leak or thin avascular bleb — a hypotonic eye is particularly vulnerable; bleb-related endophthalmitis carries poor visual outcomes
Phthisis bulbi — end-stage shrunken non-functional eye, the final consequence of untreated chronic hypotony; may require enucleation for comfort or cosmesis
Cataract progression — chronic hypotony accelerates lens opacification, particularly in eyes that were already at-risk after filtering surgery
Optic nerve dysfunction — chronic disc swelling from hypotony can leave residual nerve fiber layer thinning even after pressure is restored

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — EyeWiki Hypotony
supportResources.description: Peer-reviewed encyclopedia entry covering diagnosis and management of ocular hypotony, maintained by AAO.

supportResources.name: Glaucoma Research Foundation — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on glaucoma surgery, post-operative care, and complications including hypotony.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute — Living with Low Vision
supportResources.description: US National Eye Institute resources for patients adapting to vision loss after eye surgery or chronic ocular disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma (High Intraocular Pressure)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma is defined by elevated intraocular pressure causing optic nerve damage; ocular hypotony is the opposite — pressure of 5 mmHg or less causing structural collapse. The confusion arises because most hypotony cases follow glaucoma surgery. Goldmann tonometry distinguishes the two immediately.

confusedWithConditions.name: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (Papilledema)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Papilledema from raised intracranial pressure also causes optic disc swelling, but IOP is normal or elevated, both eyes are typically affected symmetrically, and there is no shallow anterior chamber or choroidal effusion. Hypotony produces unilateral disc swelling in a soft eye.

confusedWithConditions.name: Normal Tension Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Normal tension glaucoma is optic nerve damage at IOP in the normal range (typically 10-21 mmHg). Ocular hypotony is structural eye collapse at IOP of 5 mmHg or less. The two are distinguished by tonometry and the presence (or absence) of choroidal effusions and macular folds.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataract
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataract causes gradual painless blurring without changes in IOP. Hypotony causes blurring associated with low pressure, soft eye-feel, and structural changes on OCT. The two often coexist after intraocular surgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Age-Related Macular Degeneration
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Macular degeneration causes central vision loss from drusen, geographic atrophy, or choroidal neovascularization, with normal IOP. Hypotony maculopathy produces folds emanating from the fovea in a soft eye, and reverses with pressure restoration.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypotony is most commonly a complication of trabeculectomy, tube shunt, or other filtering procedures performed to treat glaucoma.

relatedConditions.name: Cataract
relatedConditions.relevance: Cataract surgery is a common precipitant of postoperative hypotony through clear corneal wound leaks; chronic hypotony also accelerates cataract progression.

relatedConditions.name: Age-Related Macular Degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions cause central vision loss with retinal architectural change; hypotony maculopathy is a differential when new metamorphopsia appears after eye surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Normal Tension Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Sits at the opposite end of the IOP spectrum from hypotony and shares the underlying logic that optic nerve health depends on pressure within a physiologic range.

relatedConditions.name: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension
relatedConditions.relevance: Both produce optic disc swelling; distinguishing them is part of the workup for any new disc edema in a soft or normal eye.

faqs.question: What pressure is considered ocular hypotony?
faqs.answer: Intraocular pressure of 5 mmHg or less defines statistical ocular hypotony, over three standard deviations below the population mean of about 15.5 mmHg. Clinical hypotony adds structural changes such as shallow anterior chamber, choroidal effusion, optic disc swelling, or macular folds.

faqs.question: What causes ocular hypotony?
faqs.answer: The main causes are wound leaks after cataract or filtering surgery, overfiltering trabeculectomy blebs, tube-shunt overdrainage, cyclodialysis clefts from blunt trauma, chronic uveitis with ciliary body shutdown, retinal detachment, and over-treatment with multiple aqueous-suppressant drops.

faqs.question: Is ocular hypotony serious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Brief numerical hypotony is often reversible, but sustained pressure of 5 mmHg or less risks hypotony maculopathy with retinal folds and permanent vision loss. Chronic hypotony beyond 6 months can progress to phthisis bulbi. Treatment within 2-3 months gives the best recovery odds.

faqs.question: What is hypotony maculopathy?
faqs.answer: Hypotony maculopathy is the pattern of macular folds, retinal striae radiating from the fovea, and choroidal undulation that develops when IOP stays at 5 mmHg or less for weeks. It causes blurred and distorted central vision and is reversible if pressure is restored within about 3 months.

faqs.question: How is ocular hypotony diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by Goldmann applanation tonometry showing IOP of 5 mmHg or less, combined with a slit-lamp exam, Seidel test for wound leak, gonioscopy for cyclodialysis cleft, and OCT of the macula. B-scan ultrasound is added when the fundus view is obscured.

faqs.question: What is a cyclodialysis cleft?
faqs.answer: A cyclodialysis cleft is a separation between the ciliary body and the scleral spur that lets aqueous drain into the suprachoroidal space, sustaining hypotony. It usually follows blunt trauma. Gonioscopy or UBM confirms it; treatment is laser, cryotherapy, or cyclopexy.

faqs.question: How is ocular hypotony treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment begins by stopping all pressure-lowering drops, starting atropine 1% three times daily, and patching wound leaks. Procedural options include conjunctival resuturing, autologous blood injection, scleral patch graft, cyclopexy for a cleft, and vitrectomy for chronic refractory cases.

faqs.question: Can hypotony cause blindness?
faqs.answer: Yes if untreated. Pressure of 5 mmHg or less for 3-6 months can produce permanent maculopathy, optic nerve damage, and phthisis bulbi. Most cases reaching an ophthalmologist within 3 months recover useful vision; complete blindness is uncommon when treatment is timely.

faqs.question: Can hypotony resolve on its own?
faqs.answer: Mild post-surgical numerical hypotony often resolves within 1-4 weeks as the wound heals. Once macular folds, choroidal effusion, or persistent shallow chamber appear, spontaneous resolution is uncommon and active treatment is needed.

faqs.question: What does atropine do for hypotony?
faqs.answer: Atropine 1% three times daily is a long-acting cycloplegic that deepens the anterior chamber, blocks forward rotation of the ciliary body, and reduces choroidal effusion. It is first-line topical therapy when the chamber is shallow or choroidal detachment is present.

faqs.question: What is an autologous blood injection for hypotony?
faqs.answer: Autologous blood injection is an office procedure where 0.1-0.2 mL of the patient's own venous blood is injected into an overfiltering bleb to induce a sterile inflammatory response that thickens and seals it. It resolves hypotony in 50-75% of selected cases and can be repeated.

faqs.question: What is phthisis bulbi?
faqs.answer: Phthisis bulbi is the end-stage of chronic hypotony — a shrunken, fibrotic, non-functional eye with unmeasurably low pressure. It follows severe trauma, untreated chronic hypotony, or end-stage uveitis. Vision cannot be restored; enucleation or evisceration is sometimes needed for comfort.

faqs.question: Can hypotony happen after cataract surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. A leaking clear corneal incision is the most common cause of hypotony in the first 1-2 weeks after cataract surgery. The Seidel test identifies the leak. Treatment is usually a pressure patch or bandage contact lens; persistent leaks need surgical resuturing.

faqs.question: Why does trabeculectomy sometimes cause hypotony?
faqs.answer: The filtering bleb can drain faster than the ciliary body produces aqueous, especially in young myopic eyes and when mitomycin-C is used. The result is IOP below 5 mmHg with a thin diffuse bleb. Bleb revision, compression sutures, or autologous blood injection are the usual fixes.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from hypotony take?
faqs.answer: Pressure typically rises within 24-72 hours of stopping aqueous suppressants and starting atropine. Choroidal effusions clear over 1-3 weeks. Hypotony maculopathy reverses on OCT over 4-12 weeks. After surgical revision, full visual recovery may take 3-6 months.

faqs.question: Is hypotony painful?
faqs.answer: Most patients describe a soft eye-feel, mild aching, or heaviness rather than severe pain. Sudden severe pain in a hypotonic eye is a red flag for suprachoroidal hemorrhage, endophthalmitis, or large choroidal detachment, and warrants same-day specialist review.

faqs.question: What is the Seidel test?
faqs.answer: The Seidel test is a slit-lamp procedure where a fluorescein strip is applied over a suspected wound and viewed under cobalt blue light. A green stream of dilution within 30-60 seconds confirms a leak. It is quick, painless, and the most useful bedside test in any post-surgical soft eye.

faqs.question: Can children develop ocular hypotony?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children can develop hypotony after glaucoma surgery for congenital or juvenile glaucoma, after blunt trauma, and as a complication of JIA-associated uveitis. Pediatric ophthalmology and uveitis services co-manage these cases; treatment principles match adult care plus amblyopia control.

faqs.question: How much does treatment for hypotony cost?
faqs.answer: Office measures — atropine, pressure patching, autologous blood injection — are inexpensive and usually insured. Surgical revisions such as bleb resuturing, scleral patch grafting, and cyclopexy run from several hundred to several thousand US dollars. Combined vitrectomy is at the high end.

faqs.question: Can hypotony come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence is well-described, especially in overfiltering blebs with thin avascular conjunctiva, eyes with chronic uveitis, and partially closed cyclodialysis clefts. Lifelong follow-up with attention to bleb appearance, IOP trend, and macular OCT at each visit is standard.

faqs.question: Should I avoid exercise if I have hypotony?
faqs.answer: Avoid heavy lifting, contact sports, scuba diving, and other high-impact activity during active hypotony and for 4-6 weeks after surgical revision. Gentle walking is encouraged. Full activity usually resumes 6-12 weeks after a successful intervention based on the surgeon's assessment.

faqs.question: When should I see an ophthalmologist after eye surgery?
faqs.answer: See your ophthalmologist promptly if vision drops by 2 or more lines, the eye feels suddenly soft, you notice watery discharge from the wound, you see new flashes, or straight lines look wavy. Same-day review is needed for sudden severe pain or marked vision loss.
